home.hero.title: A web terminal framework
home.hero.subtitle: Drop a fully-featured CLI into any app. Tabs, split panes, a plugin ecosystem, themes, autocomplete — everything you need, nothing you don’t.
home.frameworks.heading: Works with Your Stack
home.frameworks.subheading: Same plugin ecosystem. Same API. Native wrappers for every major framework.
home.features.plugin-ecosystem.title: Plugin Ecosystem
home.features.plugin-ecosystem.descripti: Extend functionality with drop-in plugins. 28 official plugins included, or create your own with one command.
home.features.tabs-panes.title: Tabs & Split Panes
home.features.tabs-panes.description: Multiple terminals in tabs with draggable split panes. Rename, duplicate, and manage sessions.
home.features.themeable.title: Fully Themeable
home.features.themeable.description: CSS custom properties for every surface. Match your app’s design system with zero friction.
home.features.autocomplete.title: Autocomplete & History
home.features.autocomplete.description: Tab completion, command history navigation, and inline suggestions out of the box.
home.features.multi-framework.title: Multi-Framework
home.features.multi-framework.descriptio: Native support for Angular, React, and Vue. Same plugin ecosystem, same API across frameworks.
home.features.virtual-fs.title: Virtual Filesystem
home.features.virtual-fs.description: Built-in ls, cat, mkdir, nano commands. A complete in-browser filesystem for your terminal.
home.features.piping.title: Command Piping
home.features.piping.description: Chain commands with pipes. Stream output between processors like a real shell.
home.features.config.title: Configuration System
home.features.config.description: Persistent key-value config store. Commands can read and write settings at runtime.
home.commands.heading: {{count}}+ Built-in Commands
home.commands.subheading: A complete command set out of the box — dev tools, file management, editors, and system utilities.
home.terminal.heading: Live Terminal
home.terminal.subheading: This is the real component running below. Type help to explore commands, ls to browse files, or nano to open the editor.
home.advanced.heading: Power Features
home.advanced.subheading: Beyond the basics — server connectivity, runtime extensibility, and interactive workflows.
home.advanced.server.title: Server Integration
home.advanced.server.description: Connect to a .NET or Node backend via REST or WebSocket. Execute server-side commands from the browser terminal.
home.advanced.input.title: Interactive Input
home.advanced.input.description: Yes/no prompts, confirmations, and user input flows. Build interactive CLI wizards in the browser.
home.advanced.plugins.title: Drop-in Plugins
home.advanced.plugins.description: Add new commands by importing a module. Each plugin is an npm package — install, import, and pass to the terminal component.
home.advanced.nano.title: Nano Editor
home.advanced.nano.description: A built-in text editor in the browser terminal. Create and edit files without leaving the CLI.
home.advanced.runtime-packages.title: Runtime Packages
home.advanced.runtime-packages.descripti: Install plugins at runtime directly from the terminal. No rebuild or redeployment needed — just pkg add and start using new commands instantly.
home.advanced.background-services.title: Background Services
home.advanced.background-services.descri: Run long-lived daemons and workers alongside your CLI. Supports main-thread and Web Worker execution with automatic lifecycle management.
home.plugins.heading: {{count}} Official Plugins
home.plugins.subheading: Each plugin is a standalone package. Install only what you need.
home.plugins.language-packs-desc: Translate the CLI interface into 10 languages.
home.create-plugin.heading: Create Your Own Plugin
home.create-plugin.subheading: Scaffold a complete plugin project with a single command. Generates build config, command processor, tests, and a browser-ready IIFE bundle.
home.create-plugin.step1-desc: Install the tool globally, then run create-cli-plugin — or use npx without installing
home.create-plugin.step2-desc: Add your command logic in src/lib/processors/ — define sub-commands, parameters, and use the execution context API
home.create-plugin.step3-desc: Run npx tsup to produce CJS, ESM, and IIFE bundles
home.create-plugin.step4-desc: Run npm publish --access public to publish to npm
home.create-plugin.runtime-label: Or install at runtime (no rebuild needed)
home.cta.title: Ready to add a terminal to your app?
docs.getting-started.title: Getting Started
docs.getting-started.intro: Qodalis CLI is a multi-framework terminal component for the web. It provides a fully interactive xterm.js-based terminal with a plugin architecture, built-in commands, and native wrappers for Angular, React, and Vue.
docs.getting-started.installation-desc: Choose your framework and install the corresponding package:
docs.getting-started.angular-import-desc: Import CliModule in your Angular module:
docs.getting-started.angular-styles-desc: Add the stylesheet to your angular.json styles array:
docs.getting-started.angular-template-de: Add the terminal to your template:
docs.getting-started.react-import-desc: Import and use the Cli or CliPanel component:
docs.getting-started.vue-import-desc: Import and use the Cli or CliPanel component:
docs.getting-started.vanilla-import-desc: Use the framework-agnostic CliEngine directly with any bundler (Vite, webpack, etc.):
docs.getting-started.adding-plugins-desc: Install a plugin package and add it to your modules array:
docs.getting-started.runtime-plugins-des: You can also install plugins at runtime without rebuilding:
docs.getting-started.try-it-desc: This is a live terminal running in your browser. Type help to see available commands, guid generate to create a UUID, or todo add "Buy milk" to try the todo list.
docs.getting-started.next-config: Configuration — customize options, themes, and server connections
docs.getting-started.next-core: Core Concepts — understand how commands, processors, and context work
docs.getting-started.next-plugins: Plugins — browse the full plugin catalog
docs.built-in-commands.title: Built-in Commands
docs.built-in-commands.intro: Qodalis CLI ships with {{count}}+ commands out of the box, organized into {{categories}} categories. Type help in the terminal to see all available commands, or help <command> for detailed usage of any command.
docs.built-in-commands.try-it-desc: Try any of the commands above in this live terminal. For example: json format '{"a":1}', hash sha256 hello, or theme list.
docs.configuration.title: Configuration
docs.configuration.intro: Qodalis CLI can be configured at build time through component properties or at runtime using built-in config commands.
docs.configuration.cli-options-desc: The following options can be passed to the CLI component:
docs.configuration.log-level-desc: Log verbosity level (DEBUG, INFO, WARN, ERROR)
docs.configuration.welcome-msg-desc: Custom welcome message displayed when the terminal opens
docs.configuration.server-url-desc: Server connection URL for remote command execution
docs.configuration.angular-config-desc: Pass options via the [options] input binding:
docs.configuration.angular-styles-desc: Don't forget to add the styles to your angular.json:
docs.configuration.vanilla-config-desc: Pass options directly to the CliEngine constructor:
docs.configuration.runtime-config-desc: You can view and modify configuration at runtime using the built-in config command directly in the terminal:
docs.core-concepts.command-processors.in: Every command in Qodalis CLI is powered by a command processor. A processor implements the ICliCommandProcessor interface and defines the command name, description, parameters, and the logic that runs when the command is executed.
The key fields of a command processor are:
docs.core-concepts.command-processors.fi: The command name that triggers this processor (e.g. 'greet')
Shown in help output and command listings
Nested sub-command processors (e.g. git commit, git push)
Command name to wrap/extend with additional sub-commands
docs.core-concepts.command-processors.pr: The processCommand(command, context) method is called when the user types the command. It receives the parsed command and an execution context that provides access to terminal I/O, state, spinner, and more.
docs.core-concepts.command-processors.su: Processors can define nested processors to create command hierarchies. For example, a git processor can have commit and push as sub-command processors.
docs.core-concepts.command-processors.ex: Use extendsProcessor to add sub-commands to an existing processor without modifying it. This is useful for plugins that want to extend built-in commands.
docs.core-concepts.execution-context.int: The ICliExecutionContext is passed to every command processor when it executes. It provides access to all the services and utilities you need during command execution.
docs.core-concepts.execution-context.wri: Write text, HTML, and formatted output to the terminal
docs.core-concepts.execution-context.spi: Show a loading spinner with a message
docs.core-concepts.execution-context.pro: Display a progress bar for long-running operations
docs.core-concepts.execution-context.inp: Read interactive input from the user
docs.core-concepts.execution-context.cli: Read from and write to the system clipboard
docs.core-concepts.execution-context.abo: Signal to detect when the user cancels a command (Ctrl+C)
docs.core-concepts.execution-context.not: Send notifications (info, success, warn, error) displayed in the panel status bar
docs.core-concepts.execution-context.htt: HTTP client with typed methods (get, post, put, patch, delete) that auto-cancels on command abort
docs.core-concepts.execution-context.ser: Access registered services from the dependency injection container
docs.core-concepts.execution-context.wri: The writer is the primary way to send output to the terminal.
docs.core-concepts.execution-context.ful: Commands that need full control of the terminal (games, editors, pagers) can use full-screen mode:
docs.core-concepts.execution-context.abo: Long-running commands should check context.abortSignal to respond to user cancellation (Ctrl+C):
docs.core-concepts.theming.title: Theming
docs.core-concepts.theming.intro: Qodalis CLI uses CSS custom properties for theming, making it easy to customize the terminal appearance or create entirely new themes.
docs.core-concepts.theming.built-in-desc: 29 themes are available out of the box, organized by category:
docs.core-concepts.theming.switching-des: Users can switch themes at runtime using the theme command:
docs.core-concepts.theming.css-desc: Themes are defined using CSS custom properties on the terminal container. The key properties you can customize:
docs.core-concepts.theming.custom-desc: To create a custom theme, define the CSS custom properties and apply them to the terminal container. You can scope the theme to a specific class or apply it globally:
docs.core-concepts.theming.try-it-desc: Switch themes live — type theme set dracula, theme set nord, or theme list to see all options.
docs.core-concepts.tabs-panes.title: Tabs & Split Panes
docs.core-concepts.tabs-panes.intro: The cli-panel component supports multiple terminal sessions through tabs and horizontal split panes. Users can create, rename, duplicate, split, and close terminals — all within a single collapsible panel.
docs.core-concepts.tabs-panes.tab-mgmt-d: Each tab contains one or more terminal panes. Users interact with tabs through the tab bar at the top of the panel.
docs.core-concepts.tabs-panes.new-tab-ho: Click the + button in the tab bar
docs.core-concepts.tabs-panes.rename-tab: Double-click the tab title, type a new name, press Enter
docs.core-concepts.tabs-panes.close-tab-: Click the × button on the tab, or right-click → Close
docs.core-concepts.tabs-panes.duplicate-: Right-click → Duplicate (preserves terminal state, history, and output)
docs.core-concepts.tabs-panes.close-othe: Right-click → Close Others / Close to the Right / Close All
docs.core-concepts.tabs-panes.split-desc: Within a tab, you can split the terminal horizontally to run multiple sessions side by side.
docs.core-concepts.tabs-panes.focus-how: Click inside a pane (active pane shows an accent border)
docs.core-concepts.tabs-panes.close-pane: Click the × in the pane's top-right corner
docs.core-concepts.tabs-panes.pane-width: Pane widths are distributed equally when a split is created. The minimum pane width is 10% of the panel. Closing the last pane in a tab closes the tab itself.
docs.core-concepts.tabs-panes.positions-: The panel can be positioned at four edges of the viewport. The position cycles through:
The position is persisted to localStorage and restored automatically.
docs.core-concepts.tabs-panes.config-des: The cli-panel component accepts a CliPanelOptions object to configure panel behavior:
docs.core-concepts.tabs-panes.position-d: Panel edge: bottom, top, left, right
docs.core-concepts.tabs-panes.closable-d: Show close button in panel header
docs.core-concepts.tabs-panes.sync-desc: Sync panel chrome colors with the active terminal theme
docs.core-concepts.tabs-panes.state-desc: When a tab is duplicated, the full terminal state is captured and restored in the new tab:
docs.core-concepts.tabs-panes.state-stor: Processor state stores (todo items, config values, etc.)
docs.core-concepts.input-reader.title: Input Reader
docs.core-concepts.input-reader.intro: The ICliInputReader interface provides methods for reading interactive input from the user. It is available through context.inputReader in every command processor.
docs.core-concepts.input-reader.readline: Read a line of text with optional placeholder, default value, and inline validation
docs.core-concepts.input-reader.readpass: Read input with masked characters (shown as asterisks)
docs.core-concepts.input-reader.readconf: Ask a yes/no question with configurable default, returns a boolean
docs.core-concepts.input-reader.readnumb: Read and validate a numeric value with optional min/max range and default
docs.core-concepts.input-reader.readsele: Vertical list with descriptions, groups, disabled options, and optional search
Compact horizontal picker with overflow handling for narrow terminals
docs.core-concepts.input-reader.readmult: Select multiple items with pre-checked defaults, select-all (a), and search
docs.core-concepts.input-reader.readdate: Read a date string with format enforcement (e.g. YYYY-MM-DD) and min/max validation
docs.core-concepts.input-reader.readfile: Open a file picker dialog; supports filtering by extension, multiple files, and directories
docs.core-concepts.input-reader.cancella: All input methods return null when the user cancels via Ctrl+C or Escape. Always check for null before using the result.
docs.core-concepts.input-reader.try-it-d: Try interactive input — type input-demo to walk through all input types, or yesno "Deploy?" for a quick confirmation prompt.
docs.create-plugin.title: Create Your Own Plugin
docs.create-plugin.intro: Qodalis CLI has a rich plugin architecture that lets you add custom commands. The easiest way to get started is with the scaffolding tool, which generates all the boilerplate for you.
docs.create-plugin.install-desc: Install the plugin scaffolding tool globally:
docs.create-plugin.scaffold-interactive: If installed globally, run it directly:
docs.create-plugin.scaffold-npx: Or run it without installing using npx:
docs.create-plugin.scaffold-non-interact: Or use non-interactive mode with all options:
docs.create-plugin.structure-desc: The scaffolding tool generates the following structure:
docs.create-plugin.file-processor: The command processor with your logic
docs.create-plugin.file-entrypoint: IIFE entry point for runtime installation via pkg add
docs.create-plugin.file-tsup: Build configuration producing CJS, ESM, and IIFE bundles
docs.create-plugin.implementing-desc: Every plugin command implements the ICliCommandProcessor interface. Here are the key fields and methods:
docs.create-plugin.field-description: Shown in help output and command listings
docs.create-plugin.field-parameters: Array of parameter descriptors with name, description, and required flag
docs.create-plugin.field-process-command: The handler that runs when the command is executed
docs.create-plugin.context-desc: The ICliExecutionContext passed to processCommand gives you access to all the tools you need:
docs.create-plugin.ctx-writer: Write text, HTML, errors, and warnings to the terminal
docs.create-plugin.ctx-progress: Display a progress bar for long operations
docs.create-plugin.ctx-input: Read interactive input (text, password, confirm, select)
docs.create-plugin.ctx-clipboard: Read from and write to the clipboard
docs.create-plugin.context-link: See the Execution Context page for full details.
docs.create-plugin.building-desc: The generated tsup.config.ts builds your plugin in three formats: CommonJS, ESM, and IIFE (for browser use).
docs.create-plugin.build-iife: IIFE bundle for standalone browser use
docs.create-plugin.publishing-desc: Publish your plugin to npm so others can install it:
docs.create-plugin.runtime-desc: One of the most powerful features of Qodalis CLI is runtime plugin installation. Users can install your published plugin directly from the terminal without rebuilding their application:
docs.create-plugin.runtime-detail: This downloads the IIFE bundle from npm and loads it dynamically into the running CLI. No application rebuild or deployment is required.
docs.plugin-detail.try-it-desc: Type {{command}} or help {{baseCommand}} to explore the commands.
docs.plugin-detail.not-found-desc: The requested plugin could not be found.
docs.plugins.title: Plugins
docs.plugins.intro: Extend Qodalis CLI with official plugins. Each plugin is a standalone npm package that adds new commands to the terminal.
docs.plugins.install-desc: Install any plugin via npm and add it to your modules array:
docs.plugins.runtime-desc: Or install plugins at runtime directly from the terminal without rebuilding:
docs.plugins.usage-desc: After installing, import the module and pass it to your CLI component:
docs.plugins.create-desc: Scaffold a new plugin with the CLI scaffolding tool:
docs.language-packs.title: Language Packs
docs.language-packs.intro: Qodalis CLI supports internationalization (i18n) through language pack plugins. Each language pack translates the built-in CLI interface strings (help text, error messages, prompts) into the target language.
docs.language-packs.usage-desc: Install a language pack and add it to your modules array:
docs.language-packs.runtime-desc: You can also install language packs at runtime without rebuilding:
docs.language-packs.creating-desc: Language packs are regular CLI plugins that register translations during initialization. The plugin's onInit hook receives the execution context, which provides access to the translation service.
docs.language-packs.how-1: Language packs register translations via ICliTranslationService
docs.language-packs.how-2: Use translator.addTranslations('es', translations) in the onInit hook
docs.language-packs.how-3: Translation keys follow the cli.<command>.<key> pattern
docs.server-integration.title: Server Integration
docs.server-integration.intro: Qodalis CLI can connect to a backend server to execute commands on the server side, access the filesystem, stream events, and open interactive shell sessions. The frontend CLI communicates with the backend via a standard REST and WebSocket API.
docs.server-integration.architecture-des: The integration follows a simple client-server pattern. The frontend CLI component sends commands to the backend server, which executes them and returns the output. Real-time features like event streaming and PTY shell sessions use WebSocket connections.
docs.server-integration.common-api-desc: All three backend servers implement the same API surface:
docs.server-integration.api-versions: Version discovery — supported versions, preferred version
docs.server-integration.api-execute: Execute a CLI command on the server
docs.server-integration.api-fs: Filesystem operations (ls, cat, stat, upload, download, mkdir, rm)
docs.server-integration.backends-desc: Choose the backend that fits your stack. All three implement the same API:
docs.server-integration.node-desc: Express-based server with TypeScript support, node-pty for shell sessions.
docs.server-integration.python-desc: FastAPI server with uvicorn, native PTY support via pty module.
docs.server-integration.dotnet-desc: ASP.NET Core server with Swagger UI, NuGet package distribution.
docs.server-integration.jobs-api-desc: The jobs plugin adds a REST API at /api/v1/qcli/jobs for managing background jobs. Available on all three servers when the jobs plugin is installed.
docs.server-integration.jobs-list: List all registered jobs with status and schedule
docs.server-integration.jobs-get: Get detailed information about a specific job
docs.server-integration.jobs-update: Update job options (schedule, retries, timeout, etc.)
docs.server-integration.jobs-trigger: Trigger immediate execution of a job
docs.server-integration.jobs-stop: Stop a job and cancel current execution
docs.server-integration.jobs-history: Paginated execution history with status filter
docs.server-integration.jobs-execution: Get a single execution with full logs
docs.server-integration.jobs-options-des: Each job is configured with the following options:
docs.server-integration.jobs-opt-strateg: Retry backoff: fixed, linear, or exponential
docs.server-integration.jobs-opt-overlap: Behavior when timer fires while job is running: skip, queue, or cancel
docs.server-integration.api-execute-stre: Execute a command with SSE streaming output
docs.server-integration.sse-streaming-de: All three servers support Server-Sent Events (SSE) streaming for command execution. Instead of waiting for the full response, the server streams output chunks as they are produced. The frontend CLI automatically prefers SSE streaming when the server advertises support, with transparent fallback to the legacy POST endpoint.
docs.server-integration.sse-streaming-re: The server responds with a stream of SSE events:
docs.server-integration.sse-event-output: A chunk of command output (text, HTML, table, or structured data)
docs.server-integration.sse-event-done: Command execution completed with an exit code
docs.server-integration.sse-streaming-fa: Processors that implement the streaming interface (ICliStreamCommandProcessor) emit output progressively. Non-streaming processors are wrapped automatically — their full output is sent as a single chunk followed by the done event.
docs.server-integration.admin-dashboard-: The admin dashboard is a React-based web UI that provides a visual management interface for the CLI server. It is available as a plugin for the .NET and Node.js servers and is served as a static SPA from the server itself.
docs.server-integration.dashboard-overvi: Server stats, system info, recent jobs, and live event feed
docs.server-integration.dashboard-comman: Searchable command registry browser with parameter details
docs.server-integration.dashboard-jobs-d: Job list with status, detail view, execution logs, and manual trigger
docs.server-integration.dashboard-plugin: Plugin card grid with enable/disable toggle
docs.server-integration.dashboard-filesy: File browser with upload, download, and directory management
docs.server-integration.dashboard-logs-d: Live log streaming with level filtering and search
docs.server-integration.dashboard-events: Real-time event stream and connected client list
docs.server-integration.dashboard-termin: Embedded CLI terminal panel for direct command execution
docs.server-integration.admin-dashboard-: The admin dashboard is available for the .NET server (Qodalis.Cli.Plugin.Admin) and the Node.js server (@qodalis/cli-server-plugin-admin). See the individual server guides for setup instructions.
docs.server-integration.aws-plugin-desc: The AWS plugin adds commands for interacting with AWS services directly from the CLI terminal. It supports multi-profile credential management with --profile flag on all commands.
docs.server-integration.aws-s3-desc: List buckets and objects, upload, download, delete, create/remove buckets, generate presigned URLs
docs.server-integration.aws-ec2-desc: List, describe, start, stop, and reboot instances; manage security groups
docs.server-integration.aws-lambda-desc: List functions, invoke with payload, tail invocation logs
docs.server-integration.aws-dynamodb-des: List and describe tables, scan and query items with filters
docs.server-integration.aws-sqs-desc: List queues, send and receive messages, purge queues
docs.server-integration.aws-sns-desc: List topics, publish messages, manage subscriptions
docs.server-integration.aws-cloudwatch-d: List alarms, query log groups, list metrics
docs.server-integration.aws-plugin-confi: Configure credentials per profile and use --profile to switch between environments:
docs.server-detail.node.title: Node.js Server
docs.server-detail.node.intro: The Node.js backend is an Express-based server with TypeScript support. It uses node-pty for interactive shell sessions and ws for WebSocket connections.
docs.server-detail.node.adding-processor: Register command processors using the fluent builder API:
docs.server-detail.node.docker-port: The server runs on port 8047 by default.
docs.server-detail.node.background-jobs-: Add background job scheduling with the jobs plugin. Install the package, register jobs with the builder, and mount the router.
docs.server-detail.node.sse-streaming-de: The Node.js server supports SSE streaming execution at POST /api/v1/qcli/execute/stream. Processors implementing ICliStreamCommandProcessor can emit output chunks progressively. The frontend automatically uses this endpoint when available.
docs.server-detail.node.admin-dashboard-: The admin plugin embeds a React-based dashboard for managing the server. It provides an overview page, command browser, job management, plugin toggles, filesystem browser, log streaming, event monitoring, and an embedded terminal.
docs.server-detail.python.title: Python Server
docs.server-detail.python.intro: The Python backend is built with FastAPI and uvicorn. It uses the native pty module for interactive shell sessions and FastAPI's built-in WebSocket support.
docs.server-detail.python.adding-process: Register command processors using the builder API:
docs.server-detail.python.docker-port: The server runs on port 8048 by default.
docs.server-detail.python.background-job: Add background job scheduling with the jobs plugin. Install the package, register jobs with the builder, and mount the router.
docs.server-detail.python.sse-streaming-: The Python server supports SSE streaming execution at POST /api/v1/qcli/execute/stream. Processors implementing handle_stream_async can emit output chunks progressively via FastAPI StreamingResponse. The frontend automatically uses this endpoint when available.
docs.server-detail.dotnet.title: .NET Server
docs.server-detail.dotnet.intro: The .NET backend is built with ASP.NET Core. It includes Swagger UI for API exploration and distributes as a NuGet package.
docs.server-detail.dotnet.adding-process: Register command processors via dependency injection:
docs.server-detail.dotnet.docker-port: The server runs on port 8046 by default.
docs.server-detail.dotnet.background-job: Add background job scheduling with the jobs plugin. Register jobs via the CLI builder and they are automatically scheduled as a hosted service.
docs.server-detail.dotnet.sse-streaming-: The .NET server supports SSE streaming execution at POST /api/v1/qcli/execute/stream. Processors implementing ICliStreamCommandProcessor can emit output chunks progressively. The frontend automatically uses this endpoint when available.
docs.server-detail.dotnet.admin-dashboar: The admin plugin embeds a React-based dashboard served as a static SPA. It provides an overview page, command browser, job management, plugin toggles, filesystem browser, log streaming, event monitoring, and an embedded terminal.
docs.server-detail.dotnet.aws-plugin-des: The AWS plugin adds server-side commands for interacting with AWS services. It supports S3, EC2, Lambda, DynamoDB, IAM, ECS, SQS, SNS, and CloudWatch with multi-profile credential management.
docs.server-detail.dotnet.aws-services: Supported services: S3 (ls, cp, rm, mb, rb, presign), EC2 (list, describe, start, stop, reboot, security groups), Lambda (list, invoke, logs), DynamoDB (tables, describe, scan, query), IAM (users, roles, policies), ECS (clusters, services, tasks), SQS (list, send, receive, purge), SNS (topics, publish, subscriptions), and CloudWatch (alarms, logs, metrics). Use aws configure set to store credentials and aws --profile to switch between environments.
docs.panel-api.title: Panel Programmatic Control API
docs.panel-api.intro: The CliPanel component exposes a full imperative and reactive API for external control. You can open, close, minimize, resize, manage tabs and panes, and query state — all from your application code.
docs.panel-api.live-demo-desc: Click the buttons below to control the page’s CLI panel. The log shows each method call and its return value.
docs.panel-api.interface-desc: All three framework wrappers implement this shared interface, defined in @qodalis/cli-core. The generic TEngine parameter is CliEngine from @qodalis/cli.
docs.panel-api.bindable-properties-desc: Six properties support hybrid controlled/uncontrolled mode. If you provide a binding, the panel uses your value as source of truth. If you don't, the panel manages that property internally.
docs.panel-api.prop-collapsed: Whether the panel body is collapsed (header-only visible).
docs.panel-api.prop-hidden: Whether the panel is hidden to a small edge tab.
docs.panel-api.prop-maximized: Whether the panel fills the entire viewport.
docs.panel-api.prop-activeTabId: The ID of the currently active tab.
docs.panel-api.prop-dimensions: Panel height (for top/bottom) or width (for left/right) in pixels.
docs.panel-api.angular-imperative-desc: Use @ViewChild to get a reference to the CliPanelComponent. All ICliPanelRef methods are available directly on the component instance.
docs.panel-api.angular-two-way-desc: Use Angular's banana-in-a-box syntax [(property)] for automatic two-way binding. The panel emits change events that keep your model in sync.
docs.panel-api.react-imperative-desc: Pass a ref to CliPanel. The ref exposes the full ICliPanelRef interface via useImperativeHandle.
docs.panel-api.react-controlled-desc: Pass state values as props with onChange callbacks for controlled behavior. Omit a prop to let the panel manage that property internally.
docs.panel-api.vue-imperative-desc: Use a template ref to access the exposed ICliPanelRef methods on the component instance.
docs.panel-api.vue-v-model-desc: Use v-model:property for two-way binding. Vue desugars this to :property + @update:property, which the panel emits automatically.
docs.panel-api.events-desc: Structural events are always emitted regardless of controlled/uncontrolled mode.
docs.panel-api.change-events-note: Each bindable property also has a change event, emitted on both user interaction and imperative method calls:
docs.panel-api.controlled-uncontrolled-d: Each bindable property can be independently controlled or uncontrolled. If you provide a value, the panel uses it as source of truth and emits change events for you to update. If you don't provide a value, the panel manages that state internally. This is fully backwards-compatible — existing panels with no bindings work exactly as before.
docs.panel-api.status-bar-desc: The panel header includes a built-in status bar that displays real-time information about the terminal state. It updates automatically — no configuration required. The status bar is visible when the panel position is 'bottom' or 'top'.
docs.panel-api.indicator-execution-desc: Shows 'idle' or 'running' with an animated dot when a command is executing or a fullscreen processor (e.g. a game) is active.
docs.panel-api.indicator-services-desc: Displays 'X/Y services' count. Click to open a dropdown showing each service name, status, and description.
docs.panel-api.indicator-last-command-de: Shows the name of the last executed command with a checkmark (success) or cross (error) icon.
docs.panel-api.indicator-servers-desc: Displays 'X/Y servers' count for configured CLI servers. Click to open a dropdown showing each server's name, URL, connection status, API version, and command count.
docs.panel-api.indicator-uptime-desc: Shows how long the terminal engine has been running (e.g. '5m', '1h 23m').
docs.panel-api.indicator-status-text-des: Displays notifications set by command processors via context.notifier (info, success, warn, error levels). Color-coded by severity level. Useful for showing progress during long-running operations.
docs.panel-api.status-bar-glow-desc: An animated gradient line appears below the header when a command is executing, providing a subtle visual indicator of activity.
docs.panel-api.status-bar-tab-dots-desc: Each tab shows a colored dot: green (idle), pulsing blue (running), or red (last command failed). This lets you monitor multiple tabs at a glance.
docs.panel-api.status-bar-custom-desc: Command processors can send notifications via context.notifier that appear in the status bar during execution. Notifications support four severity levels: info (purple), success (green), warn (orange), and error (red). Notifications are cleared automatically when the command finishes.
docs.panel-api.status-bar-visibility-des: In horizontal positions (top/bottom), the full status bar with text labels is shown. In vertical positions (left/right), compact icon-only indicators are displayed between the title and action buttons, with tooltips on hover for details. Services and servers indicators are clickable in both modes to show detail dropdowns.
docs.engine-api.title: Command Execution
docs.engine-api.intro: The CliEngine provides an execute() method to run commands programmatically. Combined with the Panel Control API's getEngine(), you can send commands to any terminal pane from your application code.
docs.engine-api.live-demo-desc: Type a command and click execute() to run it in the page's CLI panel. The panel will open automatically if it's not visible.
docs.engine-api.how-it-works-desc: The CliEngine is the framework-agnostic terminal engine that powers every CLI instance. Each pane in a CliPanel has its own engine. You can access it via getEngine() and run any registered command.
docs.engine-api.angular-panel-desc: Use the Panel Control API's getEngine() to execute commands in panel panes.
docs.engine-api.vanilla-desc: Without a panel wrapper, you create a CliEngine directly and call execute() on it.
docs.engine-api.api-reference-desc: Key methods available on a CliEngine instance.
docs.engine-api.method-start: Initialize the terminal, load modules, and show the welcome message.
docs.engine-api.method-execute: Run a command as if the user typed it and pressed Enter.
docs.engine-api.method-focus: Focus the terminal and fit to container dimensions.
docs.engine-api.method-snapshot: Capture a serializable snapshot of the engine state (terminal buffer, command history).
docs.engine-api.method-register-modules: Register CLI modules before calling start(). Modules provide command processors.
docs.engine-api.accessing-from-panel-des: Use the Panel Control API's getEngine() to access the engine for any pane. This works the same across all frameworks.
